blurb:
Luke's story starts with his mom, Miss Daisy, a beautiful smaller shepherd whose owner unexpectedly passed away. The boyfriend couldn't deal with Miss Daisy and her two puppies, so he took all three to the county shelter and just left them there. The county held them for weeks hoping a family member would step up. Nobody did. Finally they put out a plea for rescue help.

Luke is the fun-loving, playful one of Miss Daisy's boys. He's on the smaller side for a shepherd, and his face has that Malinois look while his mom looks more like a German Shepherd. He got lucky because he arrived right when the rescue was running their summer camp, so he's been getting tons of attention and trail walks from kids all summer long.

This is a young dog who's been through some upheaval but came out of it with his sweet, loving personality intact. He's ready for a family who won't disappear on him. You can meet Luke at Our Pal's Place in Marietta.
scoring_notes:
• Tragic family loss story creates strong emotional appeal for readers
• Young shepherd with summer camp socialization appeals to active families
• Multi-week county hold plus rescue transfer suggests moderate shelter time

blurb:
Junior's family had him for years, then suddenly decided they were allergic to him. That was their story anyway. So they dropped him at the county shelter where people kept walking past his kennel. Black dog syndrome is real, and Junior lived it.

Here's what makes Junior special: he loves ice cream dates. His favorite volunteer Katie takes him to Bruster's and Pelicans, and afterwards he goes home with her for sleepovers. In a real home, he's well-mannered, housetrained, and incredibly loving. He knows how to be someone's best friend because he was someone's best friend for years.

Junior prefers to be the only dog and does better with older kids, but if you're looking for a loyal companion who will never take you for granted, this is your guy. He's a black Lab who's been through abandonment and came out the other side still believing in people. Contact Our Pals Place to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Abandoned after years of loyalty creates strong emotional reader response
• Unique ice cream dates and sleepovers show his gentle, social nature
• Black dog syndrome awareness educates readers about adoption bias

blurb:
His name is Pork Tenderloin and he's a little misunderstood. He likes attention, but only when he's in the mood for it. He'll come find you when he wants pets, but he's not going to put up with being grabbed or overwhelmed. Some cats are like this. They're not mean or antisocial. They just have opinions about when and how they want to be touched.

He needs a calm environment and someone who knows how to read cats. If you try to force affection on him, it's not going to work. But if you let him set the pace and respect his boundaries, he's got the potential to form a real bond with you. He's not a beginner cat, but for someone who understands felines, he could be something special.

Pork Tenderloin came to Good Mews from a county shelter for a better shot at finding the right home. He needs someone cat-savvy who appreciates that the best cats aren't always the easiest ones.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Hilarious name will grab reader attention and spark conversation
• Honest portrayal of boundary-setting cat appeals to knowledgeable owners
• 'Misunderstood' status suggests longer shelter stay than typical cats

blurb:
Beignet's story is one of those that makes you wonder how a sweet cat ends up scared and alone at a county shelter. She was originally adopted from Good Mews as a kitten, but somehow found herself back in the system. The shelter reached out to Good Mews, and now she's getting her second chance.

She spent time in foster care rebuilding her confidence, and it worked. She loves being petted and is a social eater who likes company while she munches. When she wants attention, she'll sit close or lean against you. She can give gentle love bites when she gets excited, so you'll want to watch for her signals when she's had enough petting.

She's ready for a home where someone understands that cats have their own way of communicating. If you can read the room and give her space when she needs it, she's going to be a loyal, affectionate companion. Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. Submit an inquiry online to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Heartbreaking return story creates strong emotional connection with readers
• Requires experienced cat owner who can read feline body language and signals
• Foster rehabilitation success story shows resilience and second chances

blurb:
Her name is Tiny and it fits. She's petite and delicate, but don't mistake that for boring. She's curious about everything and loves exploring wherever she is. Whether she's chasing a toy, investigating a new corner of the house, or just settling in for some cozy time, she brings this gentle, playful energy with her.

Tiny came to Good Mews from a county shelter for a better chance at finding her home. She's got that perfect mix of playful and affectionate that makes cats so great to live with. Not too much energy, not too little. Just right.

If you're looking for a small cat who's going to be engaged with you and her surroundings without being overwhelming, Tiny might be exactly what you want. She's ready to find a home where she can keep exploring and being adored.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Perfect balance of playful and affectionate appeals to wide audience
• Easy-going personality makes her highly adoptable for most families
• Standard rescue story without urgency indicators suggests shorter wait

blurb:
Otis was so stressed in the overcrowded county shelter that he was urinating on himself. The moment he got out of there and into a quiet room with someone who cared, he buried his head in their lap. He just needed out. Now at Our Pals Place, he's turned into a completely different dog. He remembered how to be housetrained, how to sit politely, and how to wag his tail again. Things he was too scared to do before.

He's a mixed breed adult who adores attention and wants nothing more than to please the people around him. The volunteers say he's fun-loving now that he's decompressed. He was on the county's kill list not because anything was wrong with him, but because he fell in the wrong date range when they were full and euthanizing for space.

Whoever adopts Otis is getting a dog who knows what it's like to lose everything and will never take a good home for granted. He's going to be loyal and loving in a way that only a dog who's been through something like this can be. Contact Our Pals Place to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Dramatic transformation story from stressed to thriving creates powerful narrative
• Life-or-death urgency with kill list backstory drives immediate action
• Well-trained adult dog appeals to readers wanting skip puppy phase

blurb:
Otis was on the county shelter's kill list not because he was aggressive or sick, but because he fell into the wrong date range. When the rescue team found him, he was so stressed in that loud, overcrowded place that he was literally peeing on himself. The second they got him into a quiet room, he buried his head in someone's lap and just stayed there. He needed out, and he knew it.

Now that he's had time to decompress, you can see who Otis really is. He's a fun-loving guy who remembers how to be a dog again. House training came back to him right away, along with sitting politely and wagging his tail. He loves attention and wants nothing more than to make his people happy. The stress response he had at the county shelter isn't who he is at all.

Whoever adopts Otis is getting a dog who knows what it's like to almost lose everything and will never take a good home for granted. He's going to be the most loyal, loving companion you could ask for. You can find him at Our Pal's Place in Marietta.
scoring_notes:
• Dramatic rescue from kill list creates urgent, emotional reader connection
• Transformation story shows resilience and gratitude that tugs at heartstrings
• Decompression period and stress recovery indicates extended shelter time

blurb:
Someone named this cat Pork Tenderloin and honestly, that's already half the reason you should go meet him. He's a handsome guy who came from a county shelter for a better shot at finding the right home. He's sweet, just particular about how he likes his attention.

He'll seek you out when he's in the mood for affection, but he needs things to stay calm and low-key. He can get overwhelmed faster than some cats, so he's looking for someone who gets that cats have their own timeline. No rushing him, no forcing interactions. Just let him come to you.

He needs a cat-savvy adopter who can read his body language and respect when he's done with socializing. If you understand that some cats are just more selective about their people and their moments, he could be exactly what you're looking for. Good Mews is on Robinson Road in Marietta. Submit an inquiry to start the process.
scoring_notes:
• Memorable name creates instant reader interest and shareability
• Needs patient, cat-experienced adopter limiting potential matches
• Calm personality appeals to readers seeking low-maintenance companion

blurb:
Tiny is exactly what her name suggests - small and delicate - but she makes up for it with curiosity and energy. She came from a county shelter to Good Mews for a better chance at finding her home, and she's been making the most of her time there exploring every corner she can find.

She's got that perfect mix of playful and affectionate that makes for a great companion. She'll chase toys when the mood hits, investigate anything new, then settle in for some cozy time. She brings a gentle energy to whatever she's doing, whether it's playing or just hanging out.

If you're looking for a smaller cat who's going to be engaged with her surroundings and interested in what you're up to, Tiny could be a great fit. She's ready to find a home where she can keep exploring and being adored. Good Mews is on Robinson Road in Marietta. Submit an inquiry online to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Perfect balance of playful and affectionate appeals to wide audience
• Smaller size makes her ideal for apartment dwellers and seniors
• Straightforward personality requires minimal special considerations

blurb:
Luke's story starts with his mom, Miss Daisy, a gorgeous smaller shepherd whose person unexpectedly passed away. The boyfriend wouldn't help, no family stepped up, so Miss Daisy and her two puppies ended up at the county shelter. They waited there for weeks hoping someone would claim them. Finally Our Pals Place stepped in.

Luke is a fun-loving, playful young shepherd mix who looks more Malinois than German Shepherd like his mom. He's on the smaller side for a shepherd, which might actually be perfect if you want the personality without the giant dog. He showed up right during the rescue's summer camp, so he's gotten tons of attention and trail walks from kids who spoiled him rotten.

He's ready for his own family now. Someone who will stick around this time and give him the stable home his mom never got to finish raising him in. Contact Our Pals Place to meet Luke.
scoring_notes:
• Multi-generational tragedy with deceased owner creates compelling backstory
• Well-socialized through summer camp program appeals to families
• Smaller shepherd size perfect for readers wanting breed without bulk

blurb:
Sometimes the best stories are about second chances. Beignet was adopted from Good Mews as a kitten, but somehow ended up scared and alone at a county shelter. Good Mews brought her back to safety, and now she's ready to try again.

She went straight into foster care to rebuild her confidence, and it worked. She's sweet, loves being petted, and is a social eater who likes having company at mealtime. When she wants attention, she'll sit close or lean against you. She can give gentle love bites when she gets overstimulated, so she needs someone who knows how to read cat signals and give her space when she asks for it.

This isn't a cat with behavioral problems. This is just a cat who's been through some stuff and needs an adopter who gets it. If you understand cats and want to give a good one her second shot at a real home, Beignet might be exactly what you're looking for.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Powerful second-chance story that readers will emotionally connect with
• Sweet, social cat who just needs understanding - appeals to experienced cat owners
• Foster care and rebuilding confidence suggests extended shelter time
